finding.summary: Lead Vocal is sitting at the ceiling with effectively no headroom: sample peak 0.2 dBFS and true peak 0.8 dBTP while the fader is at unity. Integrated loudness is a quiet -20 LUFS paired with a low 12 dB crest factor, and the 1/3-octave spectrum tilts strongly downward (100 Hz at -18 dB, 1 kHz at -24 dB, 4 kHz at -30 dB). No FX are present on the track, so these readings describe the source audio directly.
finding.probable_cause: The incoming signal was likely already limited/compressed before reaching REAPER (crest factor 12 dB is tight for a raw vocal; silence_fraction is only 0.05 so the stats are not diluted by dead air). The fader at 0.0 dB passes that hot signal through unchanged, so the bus is only ~0.8 dB away from intersample clipping on conversion. The dark 12 dB slope from 100 Hz to 4 kHz suggests either proximity-effect bass buildup or a dull source capture with reduced presence/air; with only three 1/3-octave points this is suggestive, not definitive.
finding.confidence_reason: Peak and loudness numbers are concrete and consistent (true_peak 0.8 dBTP, sample_peak 0.2 dBFS, integrated -20 LUFS, silence_fraction 0.05 means the capture is signal-dense). Tonal reading is directionally clear from three bands but coarse, so it is reported as observation only.

evidence_refs.description: 0.05 — capture is signal-dense, stats are reliable
100 Hz -18, 1 kHz -24, 4 kHz -30 dB: dark tilt

proposal.reason: Gain stage is wrong: a vocal at 0.8 dBTP with the fader at unity leaves no margin for any downstream EQ, compressor, or bus. Pulling the fader back creates headroom without altering the signal's tonal or dynamic shape (a linear gain change preserves crest factor).
